authoritative_sources.title: 29 CFR 1926.20 - General safety and health provisions
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA is the primary U.S. federal construction-safety regulator. This standard defines safety programs, frequent inspections, and competent-person oversight that can block handoff and field progression.

authoritative_sources.title: 29 CFR 1926.752 - Site layout, site-specific erection plan and construction sequence
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA defines written notifications and sequencing responsibilities between controlling contractors and erectors before work begins, making it authoritative for trade-to-trade readiness and coordination boundaries.

authoritative_sources.title: After You Get a Permit
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NYC DOB is an official authority having jurisdiction and its permit-holder guidance describes permit posting, approved-plan availability, required inspections, signoff, and permit closeout steps.
used_for: permit sign-off routing and closure boundaries

authoritative_sources.title: DOB NOW: Inspections Guide: Construction Inspection Sign Off Prior to Occupancy Request
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: An official building-department inspection guide that names permit-entire status, floor readiness, objection resolution, supporting documents, and inspection scheduling rules before occupancy-related construction signoff.
used_for: inspection scheduling and occupancy-gate artifact expectations

authoritative_sources.title: GSA 184 - Construction Progress Report
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA's official federal progress-report form and instructions define how schedule status, adverse conditions, material delivery, shop-drawing submissions, substantial completion, and change-order effects are captured in construction administration.

authoritative_sources.title: GSA Project Planning Guide - Construction Turnover Documents
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA's official project planning guide defines turnover documents, O&M manuals, warranties, record drawings, final inspections, occupancy permits, and substantial-completion checkpoints that gate closeout handoffs.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Closeout package and turnover completion gating

authoritative_sources.title: Construction Quality Management for Contractors Student Study Guide
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: USACE's official contractor quality-management guide governs preparatory and initial phases, submittals, testing plans, daily quality-control reports, and deficiency or rework tracking in public construction administration.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Work-package administration and cross-team handoff packaging

workflow_stages.name: Dependency intake and prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Normalize request scope, work order, trade, permit, inspection, site-access, and owner context before any routing or closure output is produced.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Site-readiness, safety, and permit screening
workflow_stages.description: Check whether site access, area release, approved plans, permit status, temporary utilities, pre-task safety documents, and field-readiness controls are present before work is handed off.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Cross-team coordination and ownership resolution
workflow_stages.description: Identify prior owner, next owner, duplicated ownership, schedule conflicts, and required artifact completeness across GC, superintendent, project controls, permitting, inspection, safety, and trade teams.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Inspection, deficiency, and closeout blocker handling
workflow_stages.description: Preserve objections, testing records, punch-list items, turnover documents, and substantial-completion gates as blockers until the correct owner and evidence set are explicit.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Authority-boundary escalation and closure confirmation
workflow_stages.description: Escalate whenever the request drifts into licensed trade judgment, design interpretation, code interpretation, permit approval, occupancy approval, or safety-critical signoff, and confirm closure only after destination-owner acceptance is explicit.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

artifacts: work orders, scopes of work, trade assignment records, and service requests
issued permits, permit cards, approved plans, inspection requests, objection logs, and AHJ correspondence
three-week look-ahead schedules, pull plans, baseline schedules, milestone logs, owner maps, and dependency trackers
site logs, pre-task plans, AHAs or JHAs, safety observations, stop-work notices, and temporary utility readiness notes
RFIs, submittals, design clarifications, change orders, testing plans and logs, deficiency or rework lists, and punch lists
as-built drawings, O&M manuals, warranties, commissioning records, record drawings, occupancy-related inspection outputs, and turnover packages
systems_of_record: scheduling system or look-ahead planning board

allowed: Classify trade coordination requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing permit, site, inspection, schedule, safety, or closeout prerequisites.
Request the minimum tenant facts, systems-of-record links, and authoritative records required to continue safely.
Track duplicated ownership, stale handoffs, and dependency failures across permitting, inspection, field, safety, and trade teams.
Produce handoff summaries, blocker logs, routing confirmations, and closure confirmations when evidence is complete.
escalate: Permit status, inspection requirements, safety conditions, or closeout evidence are missing or materially conflict across sources of record.
The request requires permit approval, occupancy approval, code interpretation, licensed trade judgment, engineering review, safety signoff, or change-order approval.
Public guidance, tenant policy, contract terms, or jurisdictional instructions materially conflict on the next step.
A stop-work condition, objection, inspection failure, or closeout deficiency changes whether the work may proceed.
refuse: Authorize work to proceed without required permit, site-readiness, safety, or inspection prerequisites.
Approve permit signoff, occupancy signoff, code compliance, licensed trade signoff, engineer-of-record decisions, or design interpretation.
Close a handoff or closeout package without destination-owner acceptance and required artifact completeness.

domain_failure_modes: trade work is handed off without permit-card, approved-plan, area-release, or temporary-utility evidence and the crew arrives unable to start safely
inspection preparation ownership is ambiguous across superintendent, project engineer, and trade teams so the inspection window is missed
the lane marks work complete even though objections, testing records, punch-list items, or turnover documents remain unresolved
schedule pressure causes the lane to imply permit approval, occupancy signoff, code interpretation, or licensed trade signoff that it does not own
a changed site-safety condition or stop-work notice is treated as routine scheduling noise and the wrong team proceeds
tenant-specific PMIS or field-service routing is missing, so the lane guesses the next owner and drops evidence continuity

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Request that implies licensed trade or code-interpretation authority
Work is handed off without required context or artifacts
Multiple teams appear to own the same step
The lane is asked to absorb execution work instead of coordinating it
Ownership is ambiguous across multiple teams
A handoff is attempted without the required context

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Local AHJ permit, inspection, occupancy, and certificate requirements vary materially by jurisdiction, project type, and tenant delivery model.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The pack uses OSHA, NYC DOB, GSA, and USACE sources as the public baseline. Runtime must retrieve jurisdiction, permit type, tenant signoff rules, and local inspection sequence before applying tenant-specific outcomes.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Licensed trade boundaries and self-perform versus subcontract delivery rules may differ by tenant contract model and licensing environment.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The public baseline constrains the lane from assuming licensed trade authority. Runtime must retrieve tenant owner maps and trade-boundary rules before routing execution-adjacent requests.

notes: The research baseline covers workflow, evidence, terminology, and failure handling instead of only generic coordination language.
The lane stops at coordination. Permit approval, occupancy approval, code interpretation, licensed trade signoff, design judgment, and safety-critical release remain outside scope.
